reasoning_summary: The live run context is 20260421_153021_bf16_gemm_v1_24f31aab at 24.195072 ms after the steady-state shared-offset hoist. That was a real improvement of 0.198 ms over the prior regular-sibling run and it became the new clean accepted base. But the machine state barely moved: registers stayed at 196, active warps stayed at 16.61%, long-scoreboard slightly worsened from 7.15% to 7.21%, and barrier remained flat at 5.49%. So the offset-hoist was useful cleanup, not a structural answer to the remaining 2.195 ms cuBLAS gap. At this point the active 128x128 path looks locally polished enough that the next round should follow the new persistent human guidance more directly. That guidance explicitly prefers 256x128 block tiling, 64x64 warp tiling, wider/coalesced access, stronger shared-memory reuse, async copy overlap, bank-conflict avoidance, L2-aware swizzle, register reuse, and deeper Pg2s/Ps2r/stage buffering. The cleanest immediate embodiment of that guidance is the existing in-tree 256x128 pivot hot-band kernel, which is a bounded geometry change rather than a correctness-sensitive rewrite. The second choice is the higher-ceiling half-panel register-budget transplant into that same 256x128 pivot family. The third choice keeps the PTX barrier-trim family hot as an orthogonal fallback, because the accepted 128x128/PTX surface still remains the most stable reference if the 256x128 geometry family disappoints.
notes: Round 4 explicitly lets the persistent human guidance pull the ranking toward 256x128 families. The round-3 hoist win is accepted as a useful 128x128 cleanup, but it did not move occupancy or scoreboard enough to justify another small 128x128-only exploit first.

family_audit.rationale: This is the lowest-risk family that directly matches the new human guidance around 256x128 block tiling and 64x64 warp tiling, and it already exists in-tree.
This remains the highest-ceiling version of the same human-guided 256x128 idea, but it stays second because the correctness and implementation risk are much higher than the simple pivot promotion.
The latest run proved that small control cleanup on 128x128 still matters, but it did not change the occupancy-limited machine state enough to remain the top family.
The PTX barrier-trim family stays alive because it still maps cleanly onto the human guidance around async copy, staging, and shared-memory reuse on the accepted PTX surface.
The regular sibling plus hoist sequence already extracted the obvious low-risk 128x128 gains, so this family can wait behind the stronger 256x128-guided branches.
The stale 64x384 row-band family still conflicts with both the live machine-state evidence and the new human guidance.

directions.name: Promote The Existing 256x128 Pivot Hot-Band Kernel
directions.hypothesis: The 128x128 family has now delivered two incremental wins without moving achieved warps or occupancy limits. The cleanest next structural test is to route the default hot-band launch onto the existing 256x128 pivot kernel so the search directly tests the human-guided 256x128 / 64x64 tiling preference and a materially different CTA geometry on the accepted clean base.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Current four-warp 128x128 CTA geometry is capping residency and CTA-count efficiency on the hot-band region more than local pointer arithmetic is.
directions.risk: Moderate. The kernel already exists and aligns with the human tiling guidance, but a larger CTA can still lose if it bloats the live set or export path.
metrics_to_recheck: kernel name and grid size in ncu_metrics.csv
ranking_notes: This is the simplest direction that directly matches the new persistent human guidance around 256x128 tiling and 64x64 warp tiles.
It outranks dir_02 because it is already in-tree and therefore tests the geometry thesis without immediately paying the cost of a correctness-sensitive rewrite.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the 256x128 pivot kernel fails to improve achieved warps or regresses runtime materially versus the new accepted base.
expected_local_changes: Swap the default hot-band launch from the current 128x128 regular sibling to the existing 256x128 pivot kernel.
Keep the residual row-band and tail handling unchanged for the first measurement.

directions.name: Transplant The Half-Panel Register Budget Into The Correct 256x128 Pivot
directions.hypothesis: If the simple 256x128 pivot is still not enough, the stronger version of the same human-guided idea is to start from the correctness-safe 256x128 pivot and transplant the low-register staging / reuse behavior from the archived half-panel branch without reviving split-writer ownership.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Register footprint and steady-state staging efficiency inside the 256x128 hot-band path are still capping residency and latency hiding.
directions.risk: High. This is the highest-ceiling family left in the repo, but it touches the same 256x128 control/export surface that has failed correctness before.
metrics_to_recheck: correctness on all 3 dataset cases before trusting runtime
ranking_notes: This is the strongest direct match to the human guidance around 256x128 tiling, register reuse, and deeper buffering.
It ranks behind dir_01 only because the geometry-only pivot promotion is a much cleaner first test on the same idea axis.
directions.stop_condition: Stop immediately if correctness fails again or if the implementation revives the old split-writer ownership pattern.
expected_local_changes: Start from the correctness-safe 256x128 pivot launch surface.
Transplant only the low-register staging and reuse ideas from the archived half-panel branch.

directions.name: Trim Microkernel Barriers Without Reintroducing Shared-Memory Blowup
directions.hypothesis: If the 256x128 family underdelivers, the best orthogonal fallback is still to return to the accepted PTX microkernel surface and trim the single-K barrier cadence more surgically while preserving the smaller shared-memory footprint.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Barrier cadence and PTX-stage handoff overhead inside the single-K 128x128 microkernel remain an unresolved latency tax on the accepted PTX surface.
directions.risk: High. This remains PTX-microkernel surgery on the accepted surface.
ranking_notes: This stays in the top three because it is the strongest non-256x128 fallback and still matches the human guidance around async copy, reuse, and staging.
It ranks third because the new accepted 128x128 run suggests the next big move should test geometry before returning to another PTX schedule surgery.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if shared memory rises above the accepted 22,016 B footprint or if barrier stall does not improve materially when compared to the PTX accepted-base profile.
expected_local_changes: Restore the accepted PTX microkernel launch surface if needed before editing the wait/sync cadence.
Retune the single-K consumer-barrier handoff without adding a second full K-tile shared buffer pair.
